question.text: What is delta hedging in the context of options?

answer.text: Continuously adjusting the hedge ratio by trading the underlying asset to maintain a delta-neutral portfolio as the underlying price changes
Buying or selling options with a delta of exactly 1.0 to perfectly replicate the underlying
Using the delta of the portfolio to calculate the number of futures contracts needed for hedging
Hedging only when the delta of the position exceeds a predetermined threshold

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Delta hedging involves continuously rebalancing a portfolio by buying or selling shares of the underlying to keep the overall delta at zero. As the underlying price changes, option delta changes, requiring adjustment of the hedge position. **The Trap:** Delta hedging is NOT a one-time hedge — it requires continuous rebalancing because delta changes with price movement (gamma effect). **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch5 covers delta hedging for equity options. The concept applies identically to currency options (I) and interest rate options (IV). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Delta hedging in practice is costly due to frequent rebalancing — the trade-off is between hedge accuracy and transaction costs.
